coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled dendrites of graphene oxide quantum dots via bottom-up lyophilization synthesis
coredata.dc:creator: Gomaa, Islam
Hosny, Nasser Mohammed
Ibrahim, Medhat A.
coredata.dc:description: This study establishes the feasibility of bottom-up self-assembly of zero-dimensional graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) into highly ordered, multi-dimensional dendritic nanostructures (F-GOQDs) using a freeze-drying approach. The distinctive features of dendrites have been investigated using various characterization techniques. FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed the presence of carboxyl groups in the dendrite formation mechanism. UV–visible spectroscopy also revealed the oxidation process of graphite sheets, while Raman spectroscopy indicated a slightly lower density of structural imperfections in F-GOQDs than in GOQDs, as evidenced by the ID/IG ratio of 0.97 and 0.93 for GOQDs and F-GOQDs, respectively. The XRD analysis revealed that the crystallite size was 2.3 nm for the GOQDs and 5.5 nm for F-GOQDs. TEM provided insights into the hexagonal particle structure of the GOQDs with a size of 2.25 nm. FESEM and STEM were used to assess the success of the lyophilization process and the form of dendrites, which revealed an average size of 0.6 µm. Dendrite void generation in F-GOQD motifs was investigated at the molecular level using density functional theory (DFT) simulations. The results show that the correlation of the total dipole moment, electrostatic potential, and the decrease in bandgap energy produces these dendrite gaps. Moreover, the wet chemical route employed in this study holds significant potential for the synthesis of both organic and inorganic nanomaterials. The findings of this work not only advance our understanding of dendritic structure and emptiness creation but also offer important guidance for designing and creating novel nanostructures with integrated functionalities.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled dendrites of graphene oxide quantum dots via bottom-up lyophilization synthesis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study establishes the feasibility of bottom-up self-assembly of zero-dimensional graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) into highly ordered, multi-dimensional dendritic nanostructures (F-GOQDs) using a freeze-drying approach. The distinctive features of dendrites have been investigated using various characterization techniques. FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed the presence of carboxyl groups in the dendrite formation mechanism. UV–visible spectroscopy also revealed the oxidation process of graphite sheets, while Raman spectroscopy indicated a slightly lower density of structural imperfections in F-GOQDs than in GOQDs, as evidenced by the ID/IG ratio of 0.97 and 0.93 for GOQDs and F-GOQDs, respectively. The XRD analysis revealed that the crystallite size was 2.3 nm for the GOQDs and 5.5 nm for F-GOQDs. TEM provided insights into the hexagonal particle structure of the GOQDs with a size of 2.25 nm. FESEM and STEM were used to assess the success of the lyophilization process and the form of dendrites, which revealed an average size of 0.6 µm. Dendrite void generation in F-GOQD motifs was investigated at the molecular level using density functional theory (DFT) simulations. The results show that the correlation of the total dipole moment, electrostatic potential, and the decrease in bandgap energy produces these dendrite gaps. Moreover, the wet chemical route employed in this study holds significant potential for the synthesis of both organic and inorganic nanomaterials. The findings of this work not only advance our understanding of dendritic structure and emptiness creation but also offer important guidance for designing and creating novel nanostructures with integrated functionalities.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle self-assembly and bottom-up constructability are gaining traction at the moment [1,2]. It elucidates the enigma of hierarchical structure development, motivated forces that preferred architectural structures over other shapes, and captivating dynamical, reactivity, and optical features [3]. Driving motivated forces act as the creation soul of the structure, devoted to crystal growth based on various variable criteria limited by the growth conditions and crystal nature [4].The 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry is a significant recognition of the progress made in the synthesis and development of quantum dots (QDs) and is likely to accelerate the development of quantum dot-based technologies in recent years. Quantum scale materials are on the front of 6 G Era specially detectors [5]. The morphology of particle agglomerates plays an important role in improving properties such as the micro wheel array of Quantum Dots, demonstrating up to nine times more responsiveness compared with the signal of QDs films [6]. The pattern of dendrite orientation reveals fascinating mysteries about a wide spectrum of natural design, from ice crystals and brain neurons cells to galaxies in universe [7–9]. Dendritic nanoparticles (DNPs) have significant benefits over other forms, making them appealing for various applications [10–12]. DNPs have a highly branched structure, which results in a larger surface area than other forms with similar volumes. There is a universal correlation between the surface area, catalytic activity, and efficiency for the majority of chemical and physical processes [13]. Multiple active sites are provided by the branching structure, which allows increased reactivity. This enhanced reactivity can be useful in catalysis, sensing, and energy-storage applications [14]. Dendritic nanoparticles exhibit tunable properties based on their size, shape, and composition. The ability to control these parameters allows for tailoring the properties of dendritic nanoparticles for specific applications [15]; for example, controlling the branch length and density can influence catalytic activity and drug-delivery loading capacity [16]. The numerous branches provide structural integrity and prevent agglomeration, maintaining the long-term stability of the nanoparticles [17,18]. Owing to their distinct, well-defined structure and multifunctional ability, dendritic polymers have garnered considerable scientific interest [19]. Graphene Dendrite structure have ability for creating Nano pores and gaps in hierarchical structure due to branched network [20]; which can promote innovative techniques as energy forms transformation and harvesting in recent researches [21] Few studies contribute to Graphene dendritic geometry synthesis since it needs sophisticated and high-temperature processes under harsh conditions to produce a size of approximately 100 μm, Additional co-growth promoter is a must [20,22]. Dendritic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are expected to overtop graphene dendrites with comparatively large sizes. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, no previous work has investigated the dendritic morphology of pure GOQDs [23,24]. The most well-known form of graphene in zero dimensions is graphene quantum dots (GQDs).The use of GQDs in devices for bio-imaging, light-emitting, and photovoltaic applications shows great potential because they are biologically compatible, have low cytotoxicity, are intensely luminous, and disperse well in polar and nonpolar liquids. [25–29]. It is clear that the preparation of GQDs has significant drawbacks owing to the low yield and expensive methods [30]. GOQDs, a preferred form of GQDs, demonstrate a significantly distinct optoelectronic nature, mostly owing to the presence of surface oxygen-rich functional groups (e.g., carboxyl or hydroxy groups) [31]. The exact mechanism of dendritic shape formation in quantum dots (QDs) is still an area of active research, and multiple factors may contribute to this phenomenon [32–34]. However, there are some key proposed Dendrite-GOQDs formation mechanisms that have been suggested and inspired from previous articles; beginning with crystallographic anisotropy, one theory is that the dendritic shape of GQDs may be due to the preferential growth of certain crystal planes over others. This anisotropic growth can lead to the formation of branching structures as the GOQDs grow [35]. Another theory is that the dendritic shape may be due to the self-assembly of GOQDs in solution. When GOQDs aggregate in solution, they may form branched structures because the self-organization of the particles, surface functional groups, and presence of certain chemical species on the surface of GOQDs can also influence their morphology [36]. For example, oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups have been shown to promote the formation of dendritic structures in GOQDs [20,37–39]. Finally, the reaction and synthesis conditions, such as the temperature, time, and solvent used, can also play a role in determining the morphology of GQDs. For instance, a slow and controlled growth process may promote the formation of dendritic structures, whereas a fast and uncontrolled process may lead to the formation of more spherical or irregularly shaped GOQDs [40]. Notably, the exact mechanism of dendrite formation in GOQDs is likely to be complex and multifaceted and may involve a combination of these factors. Further research is required to fully understand these underlying processes. As indicated in a recent work, cyclic electron delocalization in aromatic system agglomerations gives rise to exceptional stability, equal bond lengths, and improved chemical reactivity [41]. In this work, GOQDs were prepared from a graphite powder precursor simply in one pot by the sonochemical method. DFT calculations and experimental results confirmed the stability and reactivity of the F-GOQDs. Dendritic-shaped graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), characterized by branched structures with abundant functional groups, possess remarkable properties that render them highly promising candidates for practical applications in pollution control and drug-related fields [42]. Regarding pollution control, the high surface area, tunable bandgap, and functionalization capacity of dendritic GOQDs will enable effective adsorption and catalytic degradation of a wide range of pollutants, including heavy metals, organic dyes, and persistent organic compounds [43]. The dendritic architecture facilitates increased pollutant adsorption sites and enhanced mass transport, ensuring rapid and thorough pollutant removal [44]. Furthermore, the versatile surface functional groups of dendritic GOQDs enable tailored surface modifications, facilitating selective pollutant targeting and improved adsorption specificity [45]. These attributes position dendritic-shaped GOQDs as promising materials for water and air purification systems, mitigating the harmful impact of pollutants on the environment. Dendritic graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are promising nanocarriers for drug delivery, owing to their unique structural and physicochemical properties. The various branches associated with functional groups act as surface hooks may facilitate efficient drug loading and controlled release, enhancing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing adverse effects [46]. The dendritic structure facilitates increased drug-loading capacity, allowing for higher drug payloads and improved drug stability [47]. The dendritic surface of GOQDs is densely populated with functional groups, which provide facile conjugation sites for targeting ligands. This enables active targeting and enhanced drug delivery to specific sites of interest, such as tumors. These properties make GOQDs highly promising for next-generation drug delivery systems [48].

No further purification was performed before the use of any chemical. Graphite powder (Merck, Germany, powder, purity <20 μm, synthetic), H2SO4 (Fisher Scientific, purity. ≥ 97 %), H3PO4 (Fisher Scientific, purity ≥ 98 %), HCl (Fisher Chemical, purity ≤ 37 %), and H2O2 (Fisher Scientific, purity ≤ 50 %). KMnO4 (Alfa Aesar, Purity ≥ 98 %, Germany). Deionized (DI) Milli-Q water (TDS ≤ 5ppb) was used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dots nanomaterials
In the first phase, graphene oxide (GO)
ce:section.ce:para: GO was typically synthesized from pure graphite powder using the well-known improved hammer method, along with some modifications [49]. All the synthesis steps are summarized in the systematic synthesis diagram in the graphical abstract. For 30 min, 70 ml of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and 7 ml of phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were mixed and stirred at a 10:1 (v/v) ratio. The mixed solution was then supplemented with 0.4 g of graphite powder and swirled for 24 h before being sonicated for 2 h (300 W). The mixture was then carefully added to 2.4 g potassium permanganate (KMnO4). After six hours of stirring, the solution turned dark green. 3.00 ml Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was gradually added and agitated for 30 min. This results in an exothermic reaction. Take 20 ml of this original solution and purify it with HCl and Milli-Q Water analysis, yielded 0.62 gm of dark brown powder(GO) [50].

Dots synthesis as Second phase began with well-mixing of the original solution pot for 2 h and gradually adding 2.4 gm of KMnO4 for 24 h. The reaction mixture was cooled to ambient temperature, poured into ice with 50 ml H2O2, and irradiated by ultrasound for 4 h until the mixture turned yellow. The mixture was allowed to settle before washing with Milli-Q water and decanted multiple times. The remaining solid material was thoroughly blended with 200 mL of 30 % HCl, rinsed with distilled Milli-Q water until the filtrate reached a pH of 6, centrifuged (15,000 rpm for 0.5 h), and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 48 h to obtain 0.741 g of Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (GOQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freeze dried graphene oxide quantum dots synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Following vacuum drying, an adequate amount (1 mg for 5 ml Milli-Q water) in a 300 ml F-Cup was well disseminated by sonication, followed by a 12-hour pre-freezing stage at −20 °C in a conventional freezer. The samples were then freeze-dried (−76 °C, 0.001 mbar) for 14 days using CHRIST-Alpha 2–4 LSC-basic freeze drying processing, turned to dark brown paste, and then dried in vacuum for 6 H at copper grid at 80 °C, yielding 0.748 g of freeze-dried Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (F-GOQDs).

All models were calculated using the GAUSSAIN09 [51] software, which was implemented at the Molecular Modeling and Spectroscopy Unit at the Spectroscopy Department, National Research centre, Egypt. Calculations were conducted at DFT B3LYP/6–31G(d,p) [52–54] to map the MESP molecular electrostatic potential.

The FTIR spectra were obtained using an FTIR spectrometer (Vertex 70-Bruker, Germany (range 4000–400 cm−1) based on the KBr discs technique in accordance with the precise identical weights and measuring conditions for fabrication of all samples. The spectrum of air was used as a background before each sample analysis; background and sample spectra were taken in a room with a temperature of approximately 21–23 °C, at a spectral resolution of 3 cm−1, and 32 scans were performed for each measurement. Raman spectroscopy model WiTec Alpha 300 AR with Spectral resolution 0.72 cm−1.The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples were measured using a double-beam spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 40). The phase composition and crystal structure were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using the Malvern Panalytical Empyrean model. TEM images of the samples were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (TEM), model Joel-JEM-2100, operated at 200 kV. The morphology and topology of the samples were characterized using Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) and Scanning Transmission electron microscope (STEM) (FESEM, Quattro S, Thermo Scientific) and Freeze Dryer Model: CHRIST-Alpha 2–4 LSC-basic (Condenser temperature: −85 °C ∼ 0.001 mbar). High-speed refrigerated centrifuge (Model: Hermle Z 36 HK).

Fig. 2 . The Raman spectra of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs were characterized by two prominent peaks: the D band and the G band. For both of GOQDs the D band is located at approximately 1350 cm−1 and is associated with the disorder in the sp2 carbon network and lattice defects while G band is located at approximately 1580 cm−1 and is associated with the stretching of the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the hexagonal lattice structure [74,75]. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) is 0.97 and 0.93 for GOQDs and F- GOQDs respectively. ID/IG is a good measure of the disorder in the graphene species, relatively high ID/IG ratio of 0.97 suggests a higher density of structural defects and disorder in the graphene oxide quantum dots clusters. These defects can arise due to the oxidation process used to produce GOQDs, leading to the creating oxygen-containing functional groups and breaks in the carbon lattice [76]. A slightly lower ID/IG ratio of 0.93 indicates a relatively lower density of structural imperfections in F-GOQDs. The ID/IG ratio can be used to tune the optical properties of GOQDs, such as their fluorescence quantum yield and emission wavelength [77]. Moreover, higher order modes (2D, D + G, and 2 G) also decreased in intensity and became more well-defined as GOQDs tuned to dendrite shape [78].

Fig. 3 indicate X-ray Diffraction (XRD) peaks belong to crystal structures of GOQD and F-GOQD, reveal a well-oxidized nature of this GOQDs sample which shown in (Fig. 3(a)), dominated by a peak at approximately 2θ=7.4° (111), 26.28° (002) and 43.06° (100) corresponding to a graphitic interlayer distance of d = 14.30 Å, 3.38 Å and graphitic in-plane lattice 2.03 Å respectively [36]. When compared to graphite (0.334 Å), the greater interlayer separation of molecules (3.38 Å) is related to the low value of this Bragg angle; dramatically this displacement due to rich of oxygen moiety i.e. hydroxyl, carboxylic and epoxide at the edges and on the basal plane of GOQDs together with stacking water molecules are responsible for this amount of interlayer distance [37]. Clear crystallinity is promoted by tuning the F-GOQDs architecture according to the peak positions and intensities in (Fig. 3(b)) show four diffraction peaks at 10.96°, 25.47°, 35.68°, and 42.36° that can be indexed to the (111), (400), (440), and (622) and coincide with card number 01–082–2261, with d-spacing of 8.60, 3.493, 2.514, and 2.1318 Å, respectively [16]. Full data plotted in (Pdf.S1).In addition small intense of new orientation at 2θ = 66.1, 66.9 and 77.2 corresponding to inter-lamellar d-spacing of 1.63 Å,1.39 Å and 1.23 Å regarding to anisotropic orientation of dendrite structure [38] . The average crystallite size of the GOQDs was calculated using the well-known Scherer's equation [79], as shown in Eq. (1). Using Eqs. (2) and (3), the dislocation density (δ) and micro-strain (ε) were obtained [80].

The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the most intense peaks was used to estimate the crystallite size. The estimated particle sizes for GOQDs and F-GOQDs are 2.3 nm and 5.5 nm, respectively. Because of the partial reduction and removal of the oxygen moiety in the freeze-dried sample [81], the interlayer spacing reduced, causing the peak to shift to a higher 2θ value and the d-spacing to decrease [82]. All the estimated parameters are visualized in Table 1 . The dislocation densities of the prepared materials were decreased tunneling of GOQDs amorphous stacking to ordered orientation of F-GOQDs and implies introducing a crystallographic defect in the microstructure related to particle size and crystallinity of structure; give good indication that Stability increased due to decline of crystal entropy and free energy [83,84].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images of Graphene Oxide sheets (Fig. S1) reveal a diverse array of GO pieces scattered across the sample surface. The graphene oxide pieces appeared as irregularly shaped flakes, sheets and fragments with varying sizes and thicknesses. Upon closer examination, the graphene oxide pieces exhibit a characteristic wrinkled or crumpled surface morphology due to the reduction of oxygen-containing functional groups and the restoration of some graphitic structure during the synthesis process due to the improved method and sonication [85]. STEM images (Fig. 4 (a)–(f)) show the dispersion of graphene oxide quantum dots across the copper grid after following all synthesis conditions except the freeze-drying step. The dots appeared scattered and randomly connected, meaning they are not arranged in a specific pattern or order. Instead, they are likely to be distributed in an uneven manner across the grid. GOQDs exhibited a range of sizes and shapes. It's worth noting that a variety of dot sizes agglomerate, with diameters ranging from a few nanometers to tens or hundreds of nanometers. The dots may also have irregular shapes, including round, oval, or even elongated connective forms (Fig. 4 (g)), obviously demonstrated after artistic effects to deepen the network (Fig. 4 (g)). These aggregates can occur when multiple dots come into proximity and form clusters and appear as denser regions where several dots are closely packed together.

Deep insight of GOQDs particle size and shape using HR-TEM (200 kV) in (Fig. 5 (a)–(d)), appears as dark regions that have been randomly scattered. The average particle size estimations is calculated by statistical evaluation of various particles from TEM images by using ImageJ© software indicated to hexagonal symmetry in honeycomb network and collision of quantum dots within average diameter of 2.25 nm and average area 5.56 nm dominated to many defects and imperfection of crystal frame. HR-TEM images of isolated GOQDs indicated the presence of some of disordered edges. Selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) (Fig. 5(d)) shows poly crystalline structure and d-spacing has been measured and plotted in SAED pattern which confirmed hexagonal planes of lattice fringe 0.23 nm and 0.34 nm indexed for (100) and (220) in hexagonal graphite planes (JCPDS card No 00–008–0415(Pdf.S2)) [86]. A hexagonal structure with a lattice constant of 0.23 nm was discovered, which is 10 times smaller than the lattice constant of graphene (0.246 nm) [87]. Numerous particles with hexagonal symmetry and lattice constants ranging from 0.21 nm to 0.25 nm, which agree well with previously published values [88,89]. Intercalation of oxygen moiety, specifically hydroxyl groups, in GOQDs layers may cause a change in the lattice spacing of the (100) plane [27,90]. Since, d-spacing of 0.5 and 1.2 nm indexed for (422) and (111) respectively, regarding to (JCPDS card No 00–008–0415). It's worth mentioning that TEM (Fig. 5 (c)) exhibits that GOQDs have hexagonal particles shapes with different crystallite sizes and orientations. Connected diffraction spots rise a six-sided polygon which clarified (Fig. S2) accompanied by different sets of diffraction points, due to functional groups protruding the GOQDs crystallite, which decouple the interaction between the backbones of the neighboring layer as reported before [91]. Moreover, incommensurate stacking of the GOQDs particles and contains information from many GOQDs grains [27,92]. The fuzzy edge may indicate the presence of defects and/or functional groups in molecular structure [86]. FESEM of GOQDs indicated as dark accumulated spots in (Fig. 5(e)–(h)), before Lyophilization process reveal spherical flat sheets and agglomerated crystal units in different sizes and clusters orientation's within average size around 35 nm. The particle size distribution of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) can significantly differ between TEM and SEM due to variations in sample preparation, imaging techniques, sample environment, and data analysis [93–95]. In addition to high resolution STEM (Fig. 5(i)–(k)) oriented patterns of GOQDS after freeze drying process obviously illuminated the dendrite growth orientation with good symmetrical rate of growth around the building axe with variation on unit size and symmetry within an average length of 0.6 µm (Fig. S3), These synthesized dendrites exhibit significantly smaller dimensions and remarkably uniform morphologies compared to previous works of graphene-based dendrites synthesized using harsh conditions such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD) at high temperatures Table 2 . The branches of the dendritic GOQDs are thin and elongated, with a high surface area-to-volume ratio. They can extend outward from the central core in a fractal-like pattern, forming intricate and complex structures. The branches may vary in length, thickness, and orientation, giving rise to a diverse range of dendritic shapes. The dendritic morphology of GOQDs is a result of the unique synthesis process and the self-assembly properties of functionalized graphene quantum dots [20]. Closing seen to the surface in (Fig. 5(k)) refer to multiple pore's distribution in dendrite single unit with variable size. F-GOQDs, when observed under TEM (Fig. S4(a)–(f)), dendritic GOQDs appear as small, highly branched structures with a tree-like morphology within particle size 6.2 nm in good accordance with crystallite size obtained by diffraction. The central core of the GOQD is typically spherical or ellipsoidal in shape, and from this core, multiple branches radiate in different directions, resembling the branches of a dendritic tree.

Fig. 6 Shows the ultraviolet-visible spectra of graphene specie's showed absorption band around 240 nm that arouse due to π-π∗ transitions of CC bonds of graphitic carbon and a shoulder which appeared at 300 nm due to n-π∗ transitions of C = O bonds of oxidized carbon (carbonyl groups) for GOQDs [30], which blue shifted in comparison to F-GOQDs 228 nm and 310 nm due to π-π∗ and n-π∗ respectively . GOQDs can be thought of as a highly conjugated system of benzene rings, with emission bands that are similar to those of poly-aromatic compounds in pursuant to HOMO-LUMO levels. The UV–Vis spectra of GO obtained in this work are consistent with the earlier report [55,56] . Absorbance band at 230 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and a shoulder at 303 nm agrees to the n–π* transition of the CO group. These results provide evidence that GO-layers attached with CC and CO functional groups. Fig. 6

To describe the modeling data first the way of building the studied model molecules are described. A lap of 42 carbon atoms forming graphene quantum dots termed as AHEX QGDs is indicated in (Fig. 7 (a)). AHEX QGDs epoxide with two functional groups hydroxyl and carboxyl termed as AHEX-O.OH.COOH as indicated in (Fig. 7(a)). Another model of 3 units of AHEX is termed 3.AHEX GQDS is indicated in (Fig. 8 (a)). Finally, the last model is modified as epoxide and functionalized with hydroxyl and carboxyl is termed 3.AHEX GQDS.CO.COOH as indicated in (Fig. 9 (a)). To study the functionality of the studied surfaces the molecular electrostatic potential MESP is mapped at DFT: B3LYP/6–31 G (dap) as indicated in figures (Figs. 7(a)), (8(a))., (9(a)) and (10 (a)). As previously ensured, MESP is a worthwhile tool for tracking the nucleophilicity interaction status since it shows measurements of the adjacent charges, nuclei, and electron density at certain points in time. The colors used to depict this map are listed in decreasing order in the following order: red<orange<yellow<green<blue [99,100]. As shown in figure (Fig. 7(b)). For AHEX, the middle region has less electrostatic attraction towards yellow, indicating that there is still little chance of interfering with others and signifying greater chemical equilibrium. As in figure (Fig. 8(b))., the same is noticed as AHEX forming epoxide with two functional groups, but the MESP became uniformly distributed rather than central MESP in case of AHEX. Regarding MESP in figure (Fig. 9(b)). The MESP is localized among the 3 AHEX units. (Fig. 10(b)). Indicated AHEX 3 units that functionalized with epoxide and two functional groups, the MESP is indicating dendrite voids located among the 3 units of AHEX. For the 3 connected units of graphene quantum dots (3.AHEX GQDS), the MESP shows a localized distribution among the three units. This means that the electrostatic potential is concentrated in specific regions within the graphene quantum dot structure. The localized MESP suggests a relatively uniform charge distribution within each unit and less interaction or charge transfer between the units .On the other hand, for the 3 connected units of graphene oxide quantum dots (3.AHEX GQDS.CO.COOH), the MESP indicates a dendrite gabs located among the three units. This means that the electrostatic potential exhibits a dendritic pattern, with variations in charge distribution and electric potential along the interconnected units. The presence of functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, in 3.AHEX GQDS.CO.COOH introduces additional charge redistribution and interactions between the units, leading to the observed dendritic MESP pattern. The functional groups in graphene oxide have different electronegativity's and alter the electronic structure of the system. They can induce charge polarization and affect the distribution of electron density and electrostatic potential within the quantum dot structure. The dendritic MESP pattern suggests that the functional groups play a role in the formation of interconnected pathways for charge transport or interactions between the units. Another two important physical quantities are the total dipole moment and HOMO LUMO band gap energy which calculated at DFT:B3LYP/6–31G(dope) and tabulated in Table 3 . For AHEX the TDM was 0.000 Debye with ∆E 3.474 eV. Increasing the units of AHEX into 3 units slightly increased TDM to 0.012 Debye with slight decrease in ∆E 3.064 eV. A noticeable increase in TDM is noticed as AHEX is functionalized. As a result of functionalization the TDM increase to 2.226 with decreasing ∆E into 2.496 eV . While the TDM for functionalized 3 units of AHEM is 6.090 Debye while is ∆E 2.367 eV. It was stated earlier that, increasing the value of TDM with decreeing the values of ∆E is an indicator for the reactivity of a give chemical structure with the surrounding molecules [101,102] .

It is worth noting that presence of functional groups in 3 units of graphene quantum dots epoxide with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups introduces additional energy levels within the band structure, resulting in a modification of the electronic properties compared to pristine 3 units of graphene quantum dots.

These additional energy levels can lead to a narrowing of the band gap, as observed in HOMO-LUMO energy difference ∆E 2.367 eV for the 3 connected units of graphene oxide quantum dots (Fig. 11 ). This give a good hand to formation assumption of dendrite shape mechanism starts with some nucleation, i.e. the first appearance of solid growth, in the super cooled liquid. This formation will at first grow spherically until this shape is no longer stable. This instability has two causes; anisotropy in the surface energy of the liquid-solid interface and the attachment kinetics of particles to crystallographic planes when they have formed. On the solid-liquid interface, we can define a surface energy. Which is the excess energy at the liquid-solid interface to accommodate the structural changes at the interface [36].

In this study, we successfully synthesized highly ordered, multi-dimensional dendritic nanostructures through the bottom-up self-assembly of zero-dimensional graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) using a freeze-drying technique. To elucidate the structural evolution and property changes of GOQDs and graphene oxide dendrite shape (F-GOQDs), we conducted a comprehensive characterization. FT-IR and UV-visible spectroscopy revealed the presence of stepping function groups for all carbon moieties from graphite as a precursor to graphene oxide, GOQDs, and F-GOQDs. Raman spectroscopy showed that the D and G band frequencies and intensity increased as the GOQD size increased, while higher order modes also increased in intensity and became more well-defined. XRD analysis showed that the average crystallite size of GOQDs and F-GOQDs was 2.3 nm and 5.5 nm, respectively, and this was consistent with the results obtained from TEM analysis. FESEM and STEM images clearly demonstrated the amorphous stacking of GOQDs without lyophilization and the symmetrical growth of the dendrite orientation after lyophilization. DFT calculations were performed to unveil the origins of the increasing reasons for the total dipole moment, electrostatic potential, and decreasing bandgap energy of F-GOQDs motifs. The results revealed that the dendrite void coupling at a molecular level played a crucial role in these changes. Overall, this comprehensive characterization approach shed light on the mechanism of formation, structure, and properties of dendrites, providing valuable insights for future research and potential applications in fields such as nanoelectronics, catalysis, water remediation, drug delivery, and biomedicine.
